bytes.topic: Container Fundamentals — images vs containers, layers, copy-on-write filesystem
bytes.title: Kubernetes Container Fundamentals — Images, Layers, and Copy-on-Write
bytes.body:
Every Pod in Kubernetes runs containers built from OCI images — ordered stacks of read-only layers produced by each Dockerfile instruction. When the kubelet pulls an image via containerd (the default CRI since Kubernetes 1.24), the runtime unpacks these layers into an overlay filesystem. Each layer is stored once on the node and shared across all containers referencing it — this is why pulling `nginx:1.27` the second time is nearly instant.

When a container starts, the CRI adds a single writable layer on top via OverlayFS (upperdir). Any writes — log files, temp data, config mutations — go there, leaving the shared lower layers untouched. This is copy-on-write (CoW): the file is copied up to the writable layer before modification. On Pod deletion, that writable layer is discarded entirely.

Practical consequence: writing heavily to the container filesystem (not a mounted volume) is slow due to CoW overhead and bloats the ephemeral storage quota tracked per Pod under `resources.limits.ephemeralStorage`. Always mount `emptyDir` or a PVC for write-heavy workloads. Use `kubectl describe node` to inspect image layer caching and eviction pressure on a node.
bytes.code_snippet:
# Inspect image layers pulled by containerd on a node
kubectl debug node/my-node-1 -it --image=busybox -- sh

# Inside the debug pod:
crictl images | grep nginx
crictl inspecti nginx@sha256:<digest> | jq '.info.imageSpec.rootfs'

# Check ephemeral storage usage per container
kubectl get pod my-pod -o jsonpath=\
  '{.status.containerStatuses[*].allocatedResources}'

# Set ephemeral storage limit to guard against CoW bloat
# In your Pod spec:
# resources:
#   limits:
#     ephemeral-storage: "512Mi"

bytes.topic: Dockerfile Best Practices — layer caching, multi-stage builds, minimal base images
bytes.title: Kubernetes Dockerfile Best Practices — Layer Caching, Multi-Stage Builds, and Distroless Images
bytes.body:
Every Kubernetes node pulls container images independently — a 1 GB image across 20 nodes during a rolling update is 20 GB of registry traffic, directly delaying pod scheduling and increasing node disk pressure.

**Layer caching:** Copy dependency manifests before source code. For Go, `COPY go.mod go.sum ./` then `RUN go mod download` before `COPY . .` — the dependency layer only invalidates when go.mod changes, not on every commit push.

**Multi-stage builds:** Use a builder stage with the full toolchain, then copy only the compiled binary into the runtime stage. The builder stage never reaches your registry, kubelet, or node disk.

**Minimal base images:** `gcr.io/distroless/static:nonroot` for Go binaries, `gcr.io/distroless/base` for libc-dependent apps. Distroless has no shell, package manager, or cron — a hard requirement for passing Pod Security Admission's `restricted` policy. It also eliminates noise in Trivy/Grype scans, which block image promotion in ArgoCD or Flux GitOps pipelines.

The snippet below produces a ~7 MB Go image versus ~900 MB with `golang:latest`, with a nonroot UID baked in — no `securityContext.runAsNonRoot: true` workaround needed.
bytes.code_snippet:
# Stage 1: Build
FROM golang:1.22-alpine AS builder
WORKDIR /app
COPY go.mod go.sum ./
RUN go mod download
COPY . .
RUN CGO_ENABLED=0 GOOS=linux go build -o /app/server ./cmd/server

# Stage 2: Runtime — distroless, nonroot, no shell
FROM gcr.io/distroless/static:nonroot
COPY --from=builder /app/server /server
USER nonroot:nonroot
ENTRYPOINT ["/server"]

bytes.topic: Image Security — CVE scanning, non-root user, distroless images
bytes.title: Kubernetes Image Security — CVE Scanning, Non-Root Users, and Distroless Images
bytes.body:
Every Pod you deploy is only as secure as its container image. Three practices close the most critical attack surface.

**CVE Scanning:** Integrate Trivy or Grype into your CI pipeline to block images with HIGH/CRITICAL CVEs before they reach your cluster. Use `imagePullPolicy: Always` and pair it with an OPA/Gatekeeper policy that enforces images come only from your scanned registry.

**Non-Root Users:** Set `securityContext.runAsNonRoot: true` and `runAsUser: 1000` at the Pod or container level. Add `allowPrivilegeEscalation: false` and `readOnlyRootFilesystem: true` to lock down the runtime. Kubernetes will reject containers that attempt to run as UID 0.

**Distroless Images:** Replace `ubuntu` or `debian` base images with `gcr.io/distroless/static-debian12` or language-specific variants like `gcr.io/distroless/java21`. These images ship no shell, no package manager, and no libc extras — dramatically shrinking the CVE footprint and eliminating most shell-injection pivot paths post-compromise.

Combine all three with a `NetworkPolicy` to limit blast radius if a container is compromised.
bytes.code_snippet:
apiVersion: v1
kind: Pod
metadata:
  name: secure-app
spec:
  securityContext:
    runAsNonRoot: true
    runAsUser: 1000
    seccompProfile:
      type: RuntimeDefault
  containers:
    - name: app
      image: gcr.io/distroless/static-debian12
      securityContext:
        allowPrivilegeEscalation: false
        readOnlyRootFilesystem: true
        capabilities:
          drop: ["ALL"]

bytes.topic: Kubernetes Architecture — control plane vs worker nodes, etcd, API server
bytes.title: Kubernetes Architecture — Control Plane vs Worker Nodes, etcd, and the API Server
bytes.body:
Every Kubernetes cluster splits into two planes: the control plane (brain) and worker nodes (muscle). The control plane runs four critical components: kube-apiserver, etcd, kube-scheduler, and kube-controller-manager. All cluster state — Deployments, Services, ConfigMaps — lives exclusively in etcd, a distributed key-value store using the Raft consensus algorithm. Nothing talks to etcd directly except kube-apiserver.

kube-apiserver is the single entry point for all operations. kubectl, controllers, and kubelets all communicate through it via the Kubernetes REST API. It validates and persists objects to etcd, then emits watch events to downstream components.

kube-scheduler watches for unscheduled Pods (spec.nodeName is empty) and assigns them to nodes based on resource requests, taints/tolerations, and affinity rules. kube-controller-manager runs reconciliation loops — the ReplicaSet controller ensures desired vs actual pod count matches continuously.

On worker nodes, kubelet watches the API server for Pods assigned to its node and drives the container runtime (containerd via CRI) to converge actual state. kube-proxy maintains iptables/IPVS rules for Service networking. No control-plane components run on workers by default.
bytes.code_snippet:
# Inspect control plane component health
kubectl get componentstatuses

# View raw etcd keys (requires etcdctl + cluster certs)
ETCDCTL_API=3 etcdctl \
  --endpoints=https://127.0.0.1:2379 \
  --cacert=/etc/kubernetes/pki/etcd/ca.crt \
  --cert=/etc/kubernetes/pki/etcd/server.crt \
  --key=/etc/kubernetes/pki/etcd/server.key \
  get /registry/pods/default --prefix --keys-only

# Check which node a pod is scheduled to and why
kubectl get pod <pod-name> -o jsonpath='{.spec.nodeName}'
kubectl describe pod <pod-name> | grep -A5 Events

bytes.topic: Pods & Deployments — pod lifecycle, replica sets, deployment strategies
bytes.title: Kubernetes Deployments — Pod Lifecycle, ReplicaSets, and Rolling vs Recreate Strategies
bytes.body:
A Pod is the smallest schedulable unit in Kubernetes — one or more containers sharing a network namespace and lifecycle. Pods are ephemeral: kube-scheduler places them on nodes, but they're never rescheduled if a node dies. That's why you never create bare Pods in production.

A Deployment manages a ReplicaSet, which maintains a stable set of replica Pods. When you update a Deployment's Pod template (e.g. new image tag), the controller creates a new ReplicaSet and orchestrates the transition. The old ReplicaSet is retained — `kubectl rollout undo` exploits this.

Two core strategies control this transition. `RollingUpdate` (default) incrementally replaces Pods using `maxSurge` and `maxUnavailable` — zero-downtime for stateless workloads. `Recreate` terminates all Pods before spawning new ones — useful when your app can't run two versions concurrently (e.g. DB schema migrations with backward-incompatible changes).

Tune `minReadySeconds` and `progressDeadlineSeconds` to control how fast rollouts proceed and when they're marked failed. Use `kubectl rollout status` to block CI pipelines until a Deployment converges.
bytes.code_snippet:
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: api-server
spec:
  replicas: 3
  strategy:
    type: RollingUpdate
    rollingUpdate:
      maxSurge: 1
      maxUnavailable: 0
  minReadySeconds: 10
  progressDeadlineSeconds: 120
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: api-server
  template:
    metadata:
      labels:
        app: api-server
    spec:
      containers:
        - name: api
          image: myrepo/api-server:v2.1.0
          readinessProbe:
            httpGet:
              path: /healthz
              port: 8080
            initialDelaySeconds: 5
            periodSeconds: 5

bytes.topic: Services & Networking — ClusterIP vs NodePort vs LoadBalancer, cluster DNS
bytes.title: Kubernetes Service Types — ClusterIP, NodePort, LoadBalancer, and How Cluster DNS Ties It Together
bytes.body:
Every Kubernetes Service gets a stable virtual IP via kube-proxy, but the exposure level differs sharply by type.

**ClusterIP** (default) allocates an IP reachable only inside the cluster. Pods communicate using DNS names like `my-svc.my-ns.svc.cluster.local`, resolved by CoreDNS to this VIP. Zero external exposure.

**NodePort** punches a port (30000–32767) on every node's host network interface. External traffic hits `<NodeIP>:<NodePort>`, gets NAT'd to the ClusterIP, then to a backing Pod. Useful for bare-metal dev clusters, terrible for production — exposes all nodes and bypasses cloud load balancing.

**LoadBalancer** extends NodePort by provisioning a cloud LB (AWS NLB, GCP TCP LB, Azure LB) via the cloud-controller-manager. The `.status.loadBalancer.ingress[].ip` field gets populated once provisioned. Each Service costs a separate LB — use an Ingress + ClusterIP for HTTP to avoid that.

CoreDNS handles in-cluster resolution automatically. Headless Services (`clusterIP: None`) skip the VIP entirely — DNS returns individual Pod IPs, enabling stateful workload discovery (e.g., StatefulSet replicas).
bytes.code_snippet:
# ClusterIP — internal only
apiVersion: v1
kind: Service
metadata:
  name: api-internal
spec:
  selector:
    app: api
  ports:
    - port: 80
      targetPort: 8080
  type: ClusterIP
---
# Headless — DNS returns Pod IPs directly
apiVersion: v1
kind: Service
metadata:
  name: kafka-headless
spec:
  clusterIP: None
  selector:
    app: kafka
  ports:
    - port: 9092

bytes.topic: ConfigMaps & Secrets — externalizing config, secrets encryption at rest
bytes.title: Kubernetes ConfigMaps & Secrets — Externalizing Config and Encrypting Secrets at Rest
bytes.body:
ConfigMaps and Secrets decouple configuration from container images, letting you update behavior without rebuilding. Mount them as environment variables or volumes — volumes are preferred for Secrets since changes reflect without pod restarts (with `subPath` being the exception).

Secrets are base64-encoded by default, not encrypted. Anyone with etcd access can decode them trivially. Enable Encryption at Rest by configuring an `EncryptionConfiguration` resource on the API server with an `aescbc` or `aesgcm` provider, or delegate to a KMS provider plugin (e.g. AWS KMS, Azure Key Vault) for envelope encryption — the DEK is stored in etcd, the KEK lives externally.

For GitOps-safe secret management, use Sealed Secrets (kubeseal encrypts with a cluster-public-key, only the controller can decrypt) or External Secrets Operator to sync from Vault/AWS Secrets Manager into native Secret objects. Avoid committing raw Secret manifests — even base64 values — to any repository. Use `kubectl create secret generic` with `--from-literal` or `--from-file` for ad-hoc creation, and always restrict access via RBAC (`get`/`list`/`watch` on secrets should be tightly scoped).
bytes.code_snippet:
# Enable encryption at rest in kube-apiserver
# /etc/kubernetes/encryption-config.yaml
apiVersion: apiserver.config.k8s.io/v1
kind: EncryptionConfiguration
resources:
  - resources:
      - secrets
    providers:
      - aescbc:
          keys:
            - name: key1
              secret: <base64-encoded-32-byte-key>
      - identity: {}   # fallback for unencrypted reads

# Pass to API server:
# --encryption-provider-config=/etc/kubernetes/encryption-config.yaml

# Verify a secret is encrypted in etcd:
ETCDCTL_API=3 etcdctl get /registry/secrets/default/my-secret | hexdump -C | head

bytes.topic: Resource Management — requests vs limits, QoS classes, avoiding OOMKill
bytes.title: Kubernetes Resource Management — Requests, Limits, and Surviving OOMKill
bytes.body:
Kubernetes schedules pods based on resource requests, not limits. A pod with cpu: 250m and memory: 256Mi requests gets placed on a node that has that capacity available — limits are enforced at runtime by the kernel cgroup. Get this wrong and you'll bleed performance or crash pods.

QoS classes are assigned automatically: Guaranteed (requests == limits for all containers), Burstable (requests < limits), BestEffort (no requests or limits set). Under memory pressure, the kubelet evicts BestEffort first, then Burstable, never Guaranteed.

OOMKill happens when a container exceeds its memory limit — the kernel kills it, not Kubernetes. You'll see OOMKilled in kubectl describe pod under Last State. Root cause: either the limit is too low or there's a memory leak. Use kubectl top pod --containers and Vertical Pod Autoscaler (VPA) in recommendation mode to right-size limits without guessing.

Always set memory requests == limits for critical workloads to lock in Guaranteed QoS. For CPU, limits cause throttling — not kills — so setting cpu limits too low silently degrades latency without any crash signal.
bytes.code_snippet:
apiVersion: v1
kind: Pod
metadata:
  name: api-server
spec:
  containers:
  - name: app
    image: myapp:latest
    resources:
      requests:
        memory: "256Mi"
        cpu: "250m"
      limits:
        memory: "256Mi"   # == request → Guaranteed QoS
        cpu: "500m"        # higher than request → allows burst, may throttle

bytes.topic: Health Checks — liveness vs readiness vs startup probes
bytes.title: Kubernetes Health Checks — Liveness vs Readiness vs Startup Probes
bytes.body:
Kubernetes uses three distinct probes to manage container lifecycle, and conflating them causes subtle production failures.

**Liveness** answers: is this container still alive? A failed liveness probe triggers a restart. Use it to break out of deadlocks — never to check upstream dependencies. If your app is stuck but the database is down, restarting won't help.

**Readiness** answers: should this container receive traffic? A failed readiness probe removes the Pod from Service endpoints without restarting it. This is the right place to check dependency health — connection pools, caches, downstream APIs.

**Startup** answers: has the container finished initializing? It disables liveness and readiness checks until it succeeds, giving slow-starting JVM or migration-heavy containers time to boot. Set `failureThreshold * periodSeconds` to your worst-case startup time.

Key gotcha: liveness and readiness hitting the same `/health` endpoint is an anti-pattern. A Pod that fails liveness due to a degraded dependency gets restarted in a crash loop — exactly what readiness isolation was designed to prevent.
bytes.code_snippet:
livenessProbe:
  httpGet:
    path: /healthz/live
    port: 8080
  initialDelaySeconds: 5
  periodSeconds: 10
  failureThreshold: 3

readinessProbe:
  httpGet:
    path: /healthz/ready
    port: 8080
  periodSeconds: 5
  failureThreshold: 2

startupProbe:
  httpGet:
    path: /healthz/started
    port: 8080
  failureThreshold: 30
  periodSeconds: 10

bytes.topic: Helm — chart structure, values files, templating, release management
bytes.title: Kubernetes Helm — Chart Structure, Values, and Release Lifecycle in Practice
bytes.body:
Helm packages Kubernetes manifests into charts — a directory with a Chart.yaml, a templates/ folder, and a values.yaml. Templates use Go's text/template engine with Helm-specific functions from the Sprig library. Values flow in via values.yaml, --set flags, or -f overrides, with later sources winning. Inside templates, .Values, .Release, .Chart, and .Capabilities are the four built-in objects. .Release.Name scopes resource names to prevent collisions across installs in the same namespace.

Helm tracks releases as Secrets (v3) in the target namespace, storing each revision's rendered manifests. helm upgrade --install is idempotent — install on first run, upgrade on subsequent. helm rollback <release> <revision> restores a previous Secret's state and re-applies it. Use helm diff (the plugin) before any upgrade to catch unintended drift. Subcharts live in charts/ and inherit globals from the parent's values under a matching key. Always pin appVersion in Chart.yaml and lock dependencies with helm dependency update, which writes Chart.lock — commit both.
bytes.code_snippet:
# Install with custom values, then inspect and rollback
helm dependency update ./my-chart

helm upgrade --install my-app ./my-chart \
  --namespace production \
  --create-namespace \
  -f ./my-chart/values.prod.yaml \
  --set image.tag=v1.4.2 \
  --atomic \
  --timeout 3m

# Inspect rendered manifests before applying
helm diff upgrade my-app ./my-chart -f ./my-chart/values.prod.yaml

# View release history and rollback
helm history my-app -n production
helm rollback my-app 3 -n production

bytes.topic: Storage in K8s — PersistentVolumes, storage classes, StatefulSets
bytes.title: Kubernetes Storage — PVs, StorageClasses, and StatefulSets Done Right
bytes.body:
PersistentVolumes (PVs) decouple storage lifecycle from Pod lifecycle. You rarely create PVs manually — instead, a StorageClass drives dynamic provisioning: when a PersistentVolumeClaim (PVC) is created, the provisioner (e.g. `ebs.csi.aws.com`) spins up the backing volume automatically and binds it.

StatefulSets take this further by templating PVCs per replica via `volumeClaimTemplates`. Each Pod gets its own stable PVC — `data-postgres-0`, `data-postgres-1` — that survives Pod restarts and rescheduling. The PVC is NOT deleted when you scale down; you must clean it up manually.

Key decisions:
- `accessModes`: `ReadWriteOnce` for most block storage, `ReadWriteMany` only if your CSI driver supports it (e.g. EFS, NFS).
- `reclaimPolicy: Retain` on your StorageClass for production — prevents accidental data loss on PVC delete.
- `volumeBindingMode: WaitForFirstConsumer` delays provisioning until a Pod is scheduled, ensuring the volume lands in the correct AZ.

Mismatching AZ between node and PV is the #1 cause of Pending pods in multi-zone clusters.
bytes.code_snippet:
apiVersion: storage.k8s.io/v1
kind: StorageClass
metadata:
  name: ebs-retain
provisioner: ebs.csi.aws.com
volumeBindingMode: WaitForFirstConsumer
reclaimPolicy: Retain
parameters:
  type: gp3
---
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: StatefulSet
metadata:
  name: postgres
spec:
  replicas: 2
  serviceName: postgres
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: postgres
  template:
    metadata:
      labels:
        app: postgres
    spec:
      containers:
      - name: postgres
        image: postgres:16
        volumeMounts:
        - name: data
          mountPath: /var/lib/postgresql/data
  volumeClaimTemplates:
  - metadata:
      name: data
    spec:
      accessModes: ["ReadWriteOnce"]
      storageClassName: ebs-retain
      resources:
        requests:
          storage: 20Gi

bytes.topic: Autoscaling — HPA, VPA, KEDA for event-driven scaling
bytes.title: Kubernetes Autoscaling — HPA, VPA, and KEDA for Every Workload
bytes.body: Kubernetes ships three complementary autoscalers — each solves a different problem. HPA (HorizontalPodAutoscaler) scales replica count based on CPU/memory or custom metrics exposed via the Metrics API. It reacts in seconds but can't help when your pods are under-resourced to begin with. VPA (VerticalPodAutoscaler) addresses that: it watches historical usage and adjusts `requests`/`limits` on pods, though it requires a pod restart on resize in most modes (`Auto`, `Recreate`). Neither handles event-driven workloads well. KEDA (Kubernetes Event-Driven Autoscaler) fills that gap — it installs as a CRD + controller and scales Deployments or Jobs from zero based on external queue depth, Kafka lag, Prometheus queries, or 60+ other scalers. KEDA emits a `ScaledObject` that drives an HPA internally, so they compose rather than conflict. A common pattern: VPA in `Off` mode for recommendations → tune requests → enable HPA on CPU → layer KEDA for queue-driven burst. Avoid running VPA `Auto` and HPA on the same metric simultaneously — they'll fight.
bytes.code_snippet:
# keda-scaledobject.yaml — scale a Deployment from Kafka consumer lag
apiVersion: keda.sh/v1alpha1
kind: ScaledObject
metadata:
  name: orders-processor-scaler
spec:
  scaleTargetRef:
    name: orders-processor
  minReplicaCount: 0          # scale to zero when idle
  maxReplicaCount: 50
  triggers:
  - type: kafka
    metadata:
      bootstrapServers: kafka.default.svc:9092
      consumerGroup: orders-group
      topic: orders
      lagThreshold: "100"      # one replica per 100 unprocessed messages

bytes.topic: RBAC & Namespaces — multi-tenancy, least privilege in cluster
bytes.title: Kubernetes RBAC & Namespaces — Enforcing Multi-Tenancy with Least Privilege
bytes.body:
Namespaces partition cluster resources but provide zero security isolation on their own — RBAC is what enforces it. The pattern: one Namespace per team, a ServiceAccount per workload, and RoleBindings that scope permissions to that Namespace only. Never use ClusterRoleBindings unless the subject genuinely needs cluster-wide access.

The least-privilege checklist: bind to Role (namespaced) over ClusterRole, grant specific verbs (get, list, watch) rather than wildcards, and avoid binding to the default ServiceAccount — it's shared by every Pod in the Namespace. Audit existing bindings with kubectl auth can-i --list --as=system:serviceaccount:<ns>:<sa> -n <ns> before shipping.

For multi-tenant clusters, pair RBAC with ResourceQuota and LimitRange per Namespace to prevent noisy-neighbor CPU/memory exhaustion. Use NetworkPolicy to block cross-namespace traffic by default — RBAC controls the API server, not the data plane. Combine both layers or a compromised Pod can still reach adjacent Namespaces over the network even if it can't call kubectl.
bytes.code_snippet:
# Role granting read-only Pod access within a single namespace
apiVersion: rbac.authorization.k8s.io/v1
kind: Role
metadata:
  name: pod-reader
  namespace: team-alpha
rules:
- apiGroups: [""]
  resources: ["pods", "pods/log"]
  verbs: ["get", "list", "watch"]
---
apiVersion: rbac.authorization.k8s.io/v1
kind: RoleBinding
metadata:
  name: pod-reader-binding
  namespace: team-alpha
subjects:
- kind: ServiceAccount
  name: api-worker
  namespace: team-alpha
roleRef:
  kind: Role
  name: pod-reader
  apiGroup: rbac.authorization.k8s.io

bytes.topic: Network Policies — pod-to-pod traffic rules, ingress controllers
bytes.title: Kubernetes Network Policies — Locking Down Pod-to-Pod Traffic
bytes.body:
By default, every pod in a Kubernetes cluster can talk to every other pod — a flat, fully permissive network. Network Policies let you enforce a zero-trust model using label selectors as the access control primitives.

A NetworkPolicy applies to pods matched by `podSelector`. You then define `ingress` and `egress` rules specifying which `namespaceSelector`/`podSelector` combinations are allowed, plus optional port constraints. Policies are additive — multiple policies unioned together.

Critical nuance: NetworkPolicy enforcement requires a CNI plugin that supports it (Calico, Cilium, Weave). Vanilla kubenet ignores them silently.

For ingress controllers (e.g., ingress-nginx), traffic enters from the controller's pod namespace. You must explicitly allow ingress from `namespace: ingress-nginx` or your policy will drop legitimate external traffic post-TLS termination. Use `namespaceSelector` with a label on the ingress namespace rather than hardcoding pod IPs.

Start with a default-deny-all policy per namespace, then whitelist incrementally. This surfaces undocumented service dependencies fast — treat the breakage as a dependency audit.
bytes.code_snippet:
apiVersion: networking.k8s.io/v1
kind: NetworkPolicy
metadata:
  name: allow-ingress-controller
  namespace: app
spec:
  podSelector:
    matchLabels:
      app: api
  policyTypes:
    - Ingress
  ingress:
    - from:
        - namespaceSelector:
            matchLabels:
              kubernetes.io/metadata.name: ingress-nginx
      ports:
        - protocol: TCP
          port: 8080

bytes.topic: Rolling Updates & Rollbacks — zero-downtime deployments, rollback strategy
bytes.title: Kubernetes Rolling Updates — Zero-Downtime Deployments and Rollback Strategy
bytes.body:
Kubernetes Deployments use a RollingUpdate strategy by default, replacing Pods incrementally to avoid downtime. Two critical fields control the behavior: `maxUnavailable` (how many Pods can be down simultaneously) and `maxSurge` (how many extra Pods can exist above the desired count). Setting both to 25% on a 4-replica Deployment means Kubernetes kills one old Pod and spins up one new Pod at a time — keeping at least 3 replicas serving traffic throughout.

Readiness probes are non-negotiable here. Without them, Kubernetes promotes a new Pod to the load balancer before your app is actually ready, causing dropped requests. Define a `/healthz` endpoint and configure `readinessProbe` on the container spec.

When a rollout goes wrong, `kubectl rollout undo` reverts to the previous ReplicaSet instantly — no redeployment needed. Kubernetes retains revision history (controlled by `revisionHistoryLimit`). Use `kubectl rollout history deployment/<name>` to inspect revisions and `--to-revision=N` to target a specific one. Pair this with `kubectl rollout status` in your CI pipeline to gate promotions on successful rollouts.
bytes.code_snippet:
# Trigger a rolling update
kubectl set image deployment/my-app app=my-app:v2

# Watch rollout progress
kubectl rollout status deployment/my-app

# Inspect revision history
kubectl rollout history deployment/my-app

# Rollback to previous revision
kubectl rollout undo deployment/my-app

# Rollback to a specific revision
kubectl rollout undo deployment/my-app --to-revision=3

bytes.topic: Podman — rootless containers, daemonless architecture, Docker compatibility
bytes.title: Kubernetes + Podman — Rootless Containers, Daemonless Nodes, and Cluster-Ready Manifests
bytes.body:
Since Kubernetes 1.24 removed dockershim, nodes run containers via CRI-O or containerd — not Docker. Podman fits this shift natively: it's daemonless (no root `/var/run/docker.sock`), OCI-compliant, and its rootless mode directly mirrors `securityContext.runAsNonRoot: true`.

`podman pod create` maps 1:1 to a Kubernetes `Pod` object. Once running, `podman generate kube <pod-name>` emits a valid manifest — `apiVersion`, `spec.containers`, volume mounts included. This closes the local → cluster gap without a minikube dependency.

Rootless containers run under a non-root UID on the host, mapped via `/etc/subuid`. On nodes using CRI-O, this aligns directly with `securityContext.runAsUser` and `allowPrivilegeEscalation: false`. No privileged daemon means no single compromised process can escalate to host root — a concrete security improvement over Docker's daemon model at the node level.

`podman play kube` lets you validate Kubernetes YAML locally before `kubectl apply`, using the same OCI runtime that CRI-O leverages in production. The dev loop becomes: build → `podman play kube` → fix → `kubectl apply`.
bytes.code_snippet:
# Create a local pod mirroring a Kubernetes Pod spec
podman pod create --name api-pod -p 8080:8080

podman run -d --pod api-pod --name api-container \
  --user 1001:1001 \
  ghcr.io/myorg/api:latest

# Generate a valid Kubernetes manifest from the running pod
podman generate kube api-pod > api-pod.yaml

# Validate locally with the same OCI runtime CRI-O uses
podman play kube api-pod.yaml

# Apply directly to your cluster — no manifest rewrite needed
kubectl apply -f api-pod.yaml

bytes.topic: Local Development — Docker Compose, kind, minikube, dev vs prod parity
bytes.title: Kubernetes Local Dev — kind vs minikube and Closing the Dev/Prod Gap
bytes.body:
Running Kubernetes locally exposes a fundamental tension: minikube and kind behave differently enough from production clusters that subtle bugs slip through. minikube runs a single-node cluster in a VM or container, exposing services via `minikube service` or NodePort—neither pattern exists in EKS/GKE. kind (Kubernetes-in-Docker) creates multi-node clusters using Docker containers as nodes, making it closer to production topology but still missing cloud-provider specifics like LoadBalancer controllers.

For dev/prod parity, prefer kind with a config that mirrors your production node count and labels. Use MetalLB or cloud-provider-kind to handle LoadBalancer Services locally—otherwise they stay in `<Pending>`. Load images directly with `kind load docker-image` instead of pushing to a remote registry, cutting iteration time significantly.

Docker Compose still wins for pure local service mocking but breaks down the moment you need Ingress controllers, RBAC, or CRDs. If your production workload uses HorizontalPodAutoscaler, NetworkPolicy, or PodDisruptionBudget, validate those in kind—not Compose—before merging. The closer your local cluster topology is to prod, the fewer surprises at deploy time.
bytes.code_snippet:
# Spin up a multi-node kind cluster mirroring production topology
cat <<EOF | kind create cluster --name dev --config=-
kind: Cluster
apiVersion: kind.x-k8s.io/v1alpha4
nodes:
  - role: control-plane
  - role: worker
    labels:
      tier: app
  - role: worker
    labels:
      tier: app
EOF

# Skip the registry — load image directly into cluster nodes
kind load docker-image myapp:local --name dev
kubectl rollout restart deployment/myapp

bytes.topic: Observability in K8s — logs, metrics, traces from pods
bytes.title: Kubernetes Observability — Wiring Logs, Metrics, and Traces from Pods
bytes.body:
Kubernetes exposes three observability signals, each requiring a different collection path. Logs are written to stdout/stderr by convention; kubelet captures them via the container runtime and exposes them through `kubectl logs`. For cluster-wide aggregation, deploy a DaemonSet-based log shipper (Fluent Bit is preferred over Fluentd for lower memory) that mounts `/var/log/pods` and `/var/log/containers` as hostPath volumes.

Metrics flow through the metrics pipeline. The Metrics Server aggregates CPU/memory via the resource.metrics.k8s.io API — used by HPA and `kubectl top`. For Prometheus scraping, annotate pods with `prometheus.io/scrape: 'true'` and `prometheus.io/port`. Kube-state-metrics exposes Deployment, Pod, and Node state as Prometheus metrics that the API server doesn't surface.

Traces require in-process instrumentation. Inject the OpenTelemetry Collector as a sidecar or deploy it as a DaemonSet with `hostNetwork: true`. Use the OTLP exporter in your app pointed at `$(NODE_IP):4317` — expose `status.hostIP` via the downward API. Correlate all three signals by propagating `trace_id` into structured logs and attaching pod labels via resource attributes.
bytes.code_snippet:
# fluent-bit DaemonSet volume mounts + otel sidecar env injection
apiVersion: v1
kind: Pod
metadata:
  annotations:
    prometheus.io/scrape: 'true'
    prometheus.io/port: '8080'
spec:
  containers:
  - name: app
    env:
    - name: NODE_IP
      valueFrom:
        fieldRef:
          fieldPath: status.hostIP
    - name: OTEL_EXPORTER_OTLP_ENDPOINT
      value: "http://$(NODE_IP):4317"
